1: Israel settles at Shittim and the men begin to commit sexual immorality with the daughters of Moab. The apostasy at Baal-Peor combines idolatry and sexual immorality — the twofold temptation that the wilderness generation repeatedly failed. Paul cites this incident as a direct warning to the church (1 Cor 10:8); where Israel fell, Christ stood — the second Adam who refused every temptation that the first Adam and the wilderness generation embraced (Matt 4:1–11; Heb 4:15).
2: The women invite the men to sacrifice to their gods; Israel bows down to Baal of Peor. The sequence — sexual invitation → communal meal → worship of foreign gods — follows the pattern of covenant betrayal through the table and the bed. Christ confronts this same pattern: 'You cannot serve both God and mammon' (Matt 6:24); the one who sits at the Lord's table must not also sit at the table of demons (1 Cor 10:21).
3: Israel is yoked to Baal of Peor, and YHWH's anger blazes against Israel. The verb 'yoked' (ṣāmed) suggests being bound and harnessed — Israel has been tethered to a foreign god. Christ offers a different yoke: 'Take my yoke upon you and learn from me' (Matt 11:29), the yoke of his covenant contrasted with every enslaving religious attachment.
4: God commands Moses to take all the chiefs of the people and hang them before the LORD in the sun, to turn his fierce anger from Israel. The severe judgment on the leaders — publicly executed to turn divine wrath — anticipates the cross: Christ was publicly hanged/cursed (Gal 3:13, 'cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree') to turn the fierce anger of God from his people.
5: Moses commands Israel's judges to each kill the men of his clan who have joined Baal of Peor. The chain of judicial execution — God to Moses to judges to tribes — mirrors the structure of divine judgment running through every level of human authority. Christ as the final judge will not overlook what every human court has left unaddressed (Acts 17:31).
6: An Israelite man brings a Midianite woman into the camp openly, before Moses and the whole congregation, while they are weeping at the tent of meeting. The brazen public act of covenant defiance — at the very moment of communal mourning and repentance — represents willful, high-handed sin. Christ warned that there is no sacrifice remaining for those who deliberately trample the Son of God after knowing the truth (Heb 10:26–29).
7: Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron the priest sees this and rises from the congregation, taking a spear in his hand. Phinehas acts on behalf of God's honor — a human champion of divine jealousy. His zeal for God's house is a type of Christ's own zeal: 'Zeal for your house will consume me' (John 2:17; Ps 69:9), the same consuming jealousy that drove Jesus to cleanse the temple.
8: Phinehas follows the Israelite man into the inner room and pierces both of them through with one thrust — the Israelite and the woman through her stomach. The plague stops. The execution of judgment stops the plague — a one-for-many substitution in reverse: the guilty die, the innocent live. Christ's substitution is the ultimate reversal: the innocent one dies so that the guilty might live (2 Cor 5:21); his death stops the plague of divine condemnation.
9: Those who died in the plague numbered 24,000. The staggering toll of the Peor apostasy — 24,000 dead — is Paul's primary example when warning the church: 'Nor should we commit sexual immorality, as some of them did, and twenty-three thousand fell in a single day' (1 Cor 10:8). Christ's gospel does not eliminate God's moral seriousness; the cross is the place where that seriousness was absorbed, not abandoned.
10: God speaks to Moses: Phinehas son of Eleazar has turned back my wrath from the Israelites by being jealous with my jealousy among them. The divine commendation of Phinehas — 'jealous with my jealousy' — identifies him as a vessel of God's own covenant passion. Christ is the ultimate vessel of the Father's jealous love for his people, the one whose death demonstrates both God's justice and his covenant commitment (Rom 3:25–26).
11: Phinehas turned back God's wrath so that God did not consume Israel in his jealousy. The language of propitiation — turning aside divine wrath — is the technical vocabulary of Christ's atonement. John writes: 'He is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the whole world' (1 John 2:2; Rom 3:25).
12: God announces: I am giving Phinehas my covenant of peace (brît šālôm). The covenant of peace — given to a human priest who intervened to stop wrath — is the OT's closest analogue to the new covenant established in Christ's blood. The God of peace brought back from the dead our Lord Jesus through the blood of the eternal covenant (Heb 13:20).
13: The covenant of peace will be for him and his descendants a covenant of permanent priesthood, because he was jealous for his God and made atonement for the Israelites. A perpetual priesthood granted through a zealous act of atonement — this is the exact structure of Christ's eternal high priesthood: obtained through his own death as the ultimate act of covenant zeal, maintained permanently because he lives forever (Heb 7:24–25).
14: The name of the slain Israelite man was Zimri son of Salu, a leader of a Simeonite ancestral house. The naming of the guilty party — a tribal leader who used his position to flaunt covenant law — shows that God takes personal note of the presumptuous. Christ as judge knows every name and every deed: 'There is nothing concealed that will not be disclosed, nothing hidden that will not be made known' (Luke 12:2).
15: The slain Midianite woman was Cozbi daughter of Zur, a leader of a Midianite clan. Both parties named, both equally guilty. The impartiality of divine judgment — naming the Israelite leader and the foreign leader's daughter — anticipates Christ's final judgment that respects no person: 'God shows no partiality' (Acts 10:34; Rom 2:11).
16: God commands Moses to treat the Midianites as enemies and attack them — because they treated Israel as enemies through their deceptive scheme at Peor. The command to make war against the corrupting foreign power connects to the spiritual warfare of the new covenant. Christ has come to destroy the works of the devil (1 John 3:8) and to disarm principalities and powers through the cross (Col 2:15).
17: The Midianites harassed Israel by their schemes which they practiced against them. The covert nature of the Midianite attack — using Balaam's advice and women's seduction rather than military force — mirrors Satan's primary strategy: not frontal assault but temptation, deception, and corruption from within. Christ overcame the tempter not by force but by the word of God (Matt 4:4) and the blood of his cross (Rev 12:11).
18: The closing summary names the affair of Cozbi — the conspiracy that was exposed and stopped by Phinehas on the day of the plague at Peor. The Peor incident closes with enemies named and judgment delivered. Christ's gospel similarly names every enemy of the soul — sin, death, the devil — and announces that through the cross they have been exposed, judged, and stripped of their power over those who trust in him (John 12:31; 1 Cor 15:54–57).
